1: The setting 'one day as he was teaching in the temple and preaching the gospel' (euangelizomenou) establishes the context for the authority-challenge: Jesus is engaged in prophetic-messianic activity (gospel proclamation in the temple) that provocatively implies authority over the sacred space he has just cleansed. The chief priests, scribes, and elders form the Sanhedrin's three constituent groups — the governing body (71 members) that held jurisdiction over religious matters in Jerusalem. Their challenge about authority is both a legal and theological inquiry.
2: 'Tell us by what authority you do these things, or who it is that gave you this authority?' — the two questions ('by what authority?' and 'who gave it?') address the two possible grounds of authority in Jewish religious life: the authority itself (divine commission? prophetic call? rabbinic ordination?) and its source (who authorized you?). Rabbis derived authority through ordination chains traceable to Moses; prophets derived it directly from divine commission. Jesus's cleansing of the temple without Sanhedrin authorization was an actionable usurpation of institutional authority.
3: Jesus's counter-question about John's baptism is not evasion but a genuine test of the questioners' capacity for honest inquiry. In Jewish legal tradition, answering a question with a question was recognized pedagogical technique — forcing the questioner to examine their own presuppositions before the answer would be useful. The specific counter-question about John places the Sanhedrin's authority claim in a bind: they must either affirm divine authority (and thereby establish the principle that validates Jesus's own authority) or deny it (and lose popular standing).
4: 'Was the baptism of John from heaven or from man?' — the binary of 'from heaven' (ex ouranou — a Jewish circumlocution for 'from God') or 'from men' represents the exhaustive division of religious authority claims. John's movement was publicly recognized as prophetic (7:26-28 — 'more than a prophet'); his murder by Herod Antipas had made him a popular martyr figure (9:7-9). The Sanhedrin's inability to evaluate John's authority honestly reveals their epistemological problem: their judgments are governed by political calculation rather than genuine inquiry into truth.
5: The Sanhedrin's internal deliberation ('If we say, From heaven, he will say, Why did you not believe him?') is exposed by Luke's narrative — the reader sees the cynical political calculation. They don't discuss what is true but what is advantageous to say. This is the hypocrisy Jesus has been warning against throughout the journey narrative (12:1; 13:15; 15:2; 16:14-15). Their deliberation reveals that their objection to Jesus is not theological (they can't honestly evaluate authority claims) but political (they will not risk their social position).
6: 'But if we say, From men, all the people will stone us, for they are convinced that John was a prophet' — the threat of stoning for denying John's prophetic status reflects the crowd's intense veneration of John as a martyr-prophet. Stoning was the legal penalty for blasphemy against a prophet (Deut 13:5; 18:20) and for leading Israel astray. The Sanhedrin's fear of being stoned by the crowd inverts the normal power dynamic: the supreme court fears the mob, which fears no retribution in defending the popular prophet's honor. The crowd's theological convictions create a political constraint on the ruling body.
7: 'So they answered that they did not know where it came from' — the formal ignorance-claim ('we do not know') is dishonest by the narrative's own witness (vv.5-6 showed they do have views but won't state them). Their strategic ignorance-claim dissolves the authority-challenge: if they cannot adjudicate John's authority, they lack the epistemic standing to adjudicate Jesus's authority. The counter-question has exposed their incapacity for honest theological judgment and nullified their standing to challenge anyone else's authority claims.
8: 'Neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things' — Jesus's refusal matches their own refusal: mutual non-disclosure ends the exchange in a logical impasse. But the impasse is asymmetrical: they cannot answer because they won't; Jesus will not answer because the conditions for honest dialogue (good-faith inquiry) are absent. The outcome is that Jesus has publicly exposed the Sanhedrin's bad faith without being trapped by a direct authority-claim that they could use against him.
9: The parable of the wicked tenants (parallel Mark 12:1-12; Matt 21:33-46) opens with vineyard imagery immediately recognizable as Israel from Isaiah's Song of the Vineyard (Isa 5:1-7 — 'For the vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel'). The audience (the leaders challenging Jesus, v.1) would have recognized the vineyard allegory immediately. The detailed vineyard description (Mark's version includes the hedge, winepress, and tower from Isa 5:2) is abbreviated in Luke — Luke's audience probably knew the Isaiah source well enough for sparse allusion.
10: The repeated servant-sending pattern (servant-servant-servant/son) reflects the prophetic history of Israel: God sent prophets repeatedly to receive the covenant's 'fruit' (justice, righteousness) and was repeatedly rejected. The beating and shameful treatment of the servants echoes the Deuteronomic History's account of prophet-rejection: Elijah fleeing (1 Kgs 19), Jeremiah beaten and imprisoned (Jer 20:2; 37:15), Isaiah's tradition (the Suffering Servant), and later tradition about prophets being killed (Matt 23:37; Acts 7:52; Heb 11:36-38). The parable compresses centuries of prophetic history.
11: The second servant's treatment ('they also beat and treated shamefully and sent away empty-handed') follows the same pattern as the first. The repetition establishes a pattern of escalating rejection. In Palestinian tenant-farming practice, if a landlord's agent was repeatedly refused and abused, the legal options were limited: court action was slow and uncertain in the Roman period; direct confrontation with entrenched tenants was dangerous. The owner's continued sending of agents (rather than immediate force) reflects the parable's portrayal of divine patience exhausted by repeated rejection.
12: The third servant's wounding and ejection (traumatisantes, the same verb for Jesus's being wounded in his passion, Luke 22:63-65; cf. Isa 53:5 LXX — traumatizō) prepares the transition to the son's death. The three servants (some manuscripts have more in Mark/Matthew) represent the prophetic series culminating in the decisive sending of the Son. The escalating violence against each servant (beaten → treated shamefully → wounded and cast out) mirrors the escalating rejection of God's covenant claims on Israel over its history.
13: 'What shall I do? I will send my beloved son; perhaps they will respect him' — the vineyard owner's deliberation is rendered explicitly in Luke's version, giving him an interior voice. 'My beloved son' (ton huion mou ton agapēton) uses the same phrase as the baptismal voice ('my beloved Son,' 3:22) and the Transfiguration voice ('my chosen Son,' 9:35). The parable's transparency about the son's identity is high — any hearer following Luke's Gospel recognizes the beloved son as Jesus. 'Perhaps they will respect him' (isos autou entraphēsontai) expresses hope that the ultimate communication will succeed where all previous ones failed.
14: 'But when the tenants saw him, they said to themselves, This is the heir. Let us kill him, so that the inheritance may ours' — the tenants' reasoning about killing the heir to claim the inheritance reflects a specific legal situation: if an absentee landlord died without heirs, tenants might claim the land through adverse possession (m. Bava Batra 3.3 on unclaimed property). Their deliberation ('let us kill him that the inheritance may be ours') exposes the logic of Jesus's rejection: the Jerusalem leadership kills Jesus not in ignorance but in deliberate self-interest — to preserve their own authority and position.
15: 'So they threw him out of the vineyard and killed him' — the reversal of the order in Mark (kill first, then throw out) to Luke's order (throw out first, then kill) may reflect the historical sequence of Jesus's passion: expelled from Jerusalem (the vineyard) before crucifixion outside the city walls (Heb 13:12-13 — 'Jesus also suffered outside the gate'). Luke's sequence maps more precisely onto the actual geography of Jesus's death. 'What then will the owner of the vineyard do to them?' — the question (rhetorical in Matthew/Mark) invites the audience's own verdict before Jesus delivers it.
16: 'He will come and destroy those tenants and give the vineyard to others' — the judgment on the tenants (destruction) and transfer of the vineyard ('to others') is Luke's version of the parabolic judgment that points toward both the Jerusalem leadership's destruction (70 CE) and the Gentile mission (Acts). 'When they heard this, they said, Surely not!' (mē genoito — the strongest Greek negative optative, 'may it never be!') is the audience's shocked protest — the leaders recognize that the parable is about them and recoil from its conclusion. The protest is both a character-reveal and confirmation that they understood the parable perfectly.
17: 'But he looked directly at them and said, What then is this that is written: The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone?' — the Psalm 118:22 citation (LXX — ho lithos hon apedokimasan hoi oikodomountes, houtos egenēthē eis kephalēn gōnias) recycles the Hallel Psalm already echoed in the entry acclamation (118:26, 'Blessed is he who comes'). The rejected-stone-becoming-cornerstone (or capstone) reversal follows the Lukan reversal-theology: the humble exalted, the last first, the rejected vindicated. The 'builders' who reject the stone are the Jerusalem leadership who have just heard themselves condemned in the parable.
18: 'Everyone who falls on that stone will be broken to pieces, and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him' — the stone-saying adds two outcomes to the Psalm citation. The two stone-images draw on different OT texts: falling on the stone (Isa 8:14-15 — the LORD as a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense, over which Israel stumbles and is broken) and the stone falling/crushing (Dan 2:34-35, 44-45 — the stone cut without hands that crushes the statue representing world empires). Together the two images create a double stone-judgment: stumbling against Jesus results in broken pieces; Jesus's final coming crushes all opposition.
19: 'The scribes and chief priests sought to lay hands on him at that very hour, for they perceived that he had told this parable against them, but they feared the people' — the leaders' correct interpretation of the parable against themselves confirms Luke's earlier point (v.14 — the tenants knew exactly what they were doing). Their immediate desire to arrest Jesus collides again with the crowd constraint (cf. 19:48 — the people hanging on his words). The political calculation that has governed their inaction throughout the temple week continues: public action against Jesus requires a crowd-circumventing strategy, which Judas will provide (22:3-6).
20: 'So they watched him and sent spies, who pretended to be sincere, that they might catch him in something he said, so as to deliver him up to the authority and jurisdiction of the governor' — the spy-mission reveals the strategy: since direct action was impossible, trap Jesus in a statement that Rome would act on. The specifically Roman legal route ('deliver to the authority of the governor') identifies the target charge: something Rome would prosecute (rebellion, tax evasion, royal pretension). The 'sincere' (dikaioi — literally 'just men') pretense is the same hypocrisy Jesus has repeatedly denounced in the journey narrative.
21: 'Teacher, we know that you speak and teach rightly, and show no partiality, but truly teach the way of God' — the flattering introduction is transparent manipulation but contains accurate theological description: Jesus does not accept prosōpolēmpsia (face-receiving, partiality based on social status) — a consistent Lukan theme (cf. Acts 10:34). The spies' accurate description of Jesus's impartiality creates the rhetorical setup for the trap: if you are truly impartial, you cannot hedge on the tax question based on political considerations.
22: 'Is it lawful for us to give tribute to Caesar, or not?' — the question (phoros — poll tax, the personal tribute paid to Rome) was one of the most politically charged questions in first-century Judea. The Zealot movement (founded by Judas the Galilean, 6 CE) explicitly rejected the census and poll tax as incompatible with God's sovereignty over Israel (Josephus, Ant. 18.4-10). The Pharisees (who valued compliance with Torah over political resistance) were generally more accommodating. Any answer creates a problem: 'yes' offends Jewish nationalists; 'no' constitutes sedition before Roman law.
23: 'But he perceived their craftiness' (panourgia — cunning/craftiness, used in the LXX for the serpent of Gen 3:1 and in 2 Cor 11:3 for Satan's deception of Eve) — Luke identifies the spies' technique with the language of Satanic deception. The term situates the trap within the cosmic conflict between Jesus and the adversary (cf. 4:1-13 where the devil tempts Jesus; 22:3 where Satan enters Judas). The trap is not merely a political maneuver but part of the cosmic adversarial campaign against Jesus throughout Luke's narrative.
24: 'Show me a denarius. Whose likeness and inscription does it have?' — the request for a denarius brings a Roman silver coin into the temple context. The denarius bore the image of the current emperor (Tiberius in Jesus's time) with the Latin inscription 'Ti[berius] Caesar Divi Aug[usti] F[ilius] Augustus' (Tiberius Caesar, Son of the Divine Augustus) — a coin that claimed both imperial authority and quasi-divine status for the emperor. That someone in the crowd immediately produces such a coin is itself significant: Roman coins circulating in the temple complex.
25: 'Then render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's' — the answer is neither sedition nor Roman collaboration but a sophisticated two-kingdom distinction. 'Render to Caesar' acknowledges that Caesar's image on the coin establishes his claim — if you use Caesar's currency, you participate in Caesar's economic system and can render tax. 'Render to God' asserts a separate and superior claim: what bears God's image (humanity, made in God's image, Gen 1:26-27) belongs to God. The answer outwits the trap by establishing a principled two-domain framework rather than picking either horn of the dilemma.
26: 'And they were not able in the presence of the people to catch him in what he said, but marveling at his answer they became silent' — the spies' failure is total: not only was the trap escaped, but the answer generated wonder (thaumasantes) even among those who came to entrap. The marveling-into-silence (esigēsan — they fell silent) is the characteristic response to defeat in ancient rhetorical combat. In honor-shame culture, public rhetorical victory was a significant social achievement; Jesus has won decisively before witnesses, further enhancing his public standing.
27: The Sadducees' appearance at this point — they 'deny that there is a resurrection' (anastasis mē einai) — is confirmed by multiple ancient sources: Josephus (Ant. 18.16 — Sadducees say souls die with bodies), Acts 23:8 (Sadducees say there is no resurrection, angel, or spirit), and rabbinic traditions. The Sadducees accepted only the Torah's written text (Pentateuch) as binding, rejecting the Pharisaic oral tradition. Their resurrection-denial was based on the Torah's relative silence on personal resurrection and the Deuteronomy laws about levirate marriage, which they believed rendered resurrection internally inconsistent with Torah.
28: The levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5-10 — a brother must marry his dead brother's childless widow to preserve the family line) was a longstanding Israelite institution also attested in Genesis 38 (Judah and Tamar). The Sadducees' question constructs a reductio ad absurdum scenario: seven brothers, one wife — whose wife at the resurrection? The scenario uses the multiplication of levirate marriages to show the absurdity (as they see it) of resurrection: the social institution of marriage, which Torah regulates, would be reduced to chaos by resurrection's requirement.
29: The seven-brothers scenario (vv.29-32) is a philosophical thought experiment in the Jewish tradition of using extreme cases to test principles (a form of the rabbinic gezera shawa or kal wahomer argument pushed to its limit). Seven brothers sequentially marrying one woman and all dying without children is maximally improbable but logically possible. The scenario is designed to expose what the Sadducees see as the absurdity of resurrection belief when mapped onto Torah's social legislation. The number seven (the number of completeness) makes the case as comprehensive as possible.
30: The second brother's marriage 'took the woman as wife' — the formulaic repetition through verses 29-32 creates a literary monotony that mirrors the Sadducees' philosophical point: seven iterations of the same institution, seven times the same result, seven different potential resurrection-claims. The repetition is rhetorically deliberate: by the time the seventh brother is reached, the absurdity has been maximized and the dilemma seems insoluble.
31: The escalating repetition through the third, fourth, fifth, sixth, and seventh brothers is typically abbreviated in commentaries but serves the rhetorical purpose of the Sadducean argument: the problem compounds with each iteration. In the Sadducean framework, each successive marriage creates a new claim that cannot be adjudicated in a resurrection scenario without rendering Torah's levirate institution meaningless or absurd. Their point is that Torah's legislation presupposes the non-resurrection of the dead — resurrection would create legally insoluble conflicts within Torah's own framework.
32: 'Finally, the woman died too' — the woman's death closes the scenario and moves to the resurrection dilemma. The Sadducees have constructed a closed logical universe: seven legal marriages under Torah law, all parties deceased, all claims equally valid. 'In the resurrection, therefore, whose wife will the woman be?' Their question assumes that resurrection would simply reinstate the earthly social structure — the same marriages, the same property rights, the same family structures. Jesus's answer will challenge this assumption as the key logical error.
33: 'In the resurrection, therefore, whose wife will the woman be?' — the Sadducees' question assumes the continuity of earthly social institutions into the resurrection state. This assumption — that resurrection = resuscitation of earthly life — is the philosophical premise Jesus will refute. Jewish resurrection theology in the Pharisaic tradition (2 Macc 7; 1 Enoch 103-104; 4 Ezra 7:97; b. Sanhedrin 90b-91a) actually held a range of views on the continuity of earthly relationships in the resurrection, making Jesus's answer a contribution to an ongoing intra-Jewish debate.
34: 'The sons of this age marry and are given in marriage' — the distinction between 'this age' (ho aiōn houtos) and 'that age' (ho aiōn ekeinos) is a standard Jewish apocalyptic framework (Sifre Deut 32; b. Berakhot 34b; Mishnah Avot 4.17). This age is characterized by marriage (necessary for human reproduction and continuation); the age to come transcends this necessity. Jesus accepts the apocalyptic two-age framework and uses it to answer the Sadducees: they have confused this age's structures with that age's reality.
35: 'But those who are considered worthy to attain to that age and to the resurrection from the dead neither marry nor are given in marriage' — resurrection-life transcends the social institutions of this age. The Sadducean trap assumed resurrection would reinstantiate this age's marriage structure; Jesus denies this. The phrase 'considered worthy' (kataxiōthentes) introduces an element of divine judgment-at-resurrection that is consistent with Jewish apocalyptic but absent from the Sadducean (non-resurrection) framework. The resurrection state is qualitatively different, not merely a continuation.
36: 'For they cannot die anymore, because they are equal to angels and are sons of God, being sons of the resurrection' — the angel-comparison addresses the reproduction-necessity argument: marriage exists in this age for procreation (and thus for covenant community continuity). Angels, who cannot die, require neither reproduction nor the marriage institution that serves it. The resurrection-state's angelomorphic character (becoming 'equal to angels' — isangeloi, a term found only here in the NT) draws on Jewish traditions about righteous humans being elevated to angelic status at resurrection (1 Enoch 104:2-6; 2 Bar 51:5-10; T. Job 18.4).
37: 'But that the dead are raised, even Moses showed, in the passage about the bush, where he calls the Lord the God of Abraham and the God of Isaac and the God of Jacob' — the appeal to Exodus 3:6 (God's self-identification to Moses at the burning bush) is exegetically brilliant because it meets the Sadducees on their own ground: the Torah text (not the prophets or the writings). God's use of present tense ('I am the God of Abraham') in a text from centuries after the patriarchs' deaths implies their continued existence — past-tense 'I was the God of Abraham' would be more natural if they were simply dead.
38: 'Now he is not God of the dead, but of the living, for all live to him' — the theological principle that concludes the argument: God's covenantal relationship with the patriarchs cannot be merely historical (with the now-dead). God, who is the God of the living not the dead, must maintain living relationship with those in covenant with him — implying their continued existence. The phrase 'all live to him' (pantes gar autō zōsin) expresses the Jewish theological conviction that all people are alive before God's eternal perspective, even those who have died from the human temporal perspective.
39: 'Then some of the scribes answered, Teacher, you have spoken well' — the scribal approval of Jesus's answer in the resurrection debate is surprising: scribes were more aligned with Pharisaic than Sadducean theology, and thus would appreciate Jesus's argument for resurrection from Torah. The response 'you have spoken well' (kalōs eipas) is formal approval of a rhetorical and exegetical argument — the scribes award Jesus the dialectical victory over the Sadducees in this round of temple controversy. After this, no one dared ask him another question (v.40).
40: 'For they no longer dared to ask him any question' — the three-stage controversy sequence (authority challenge, Caesar question, resurrection question) has exhausted the Sanhedrin's ability to trap Jesus. Each attempt has resulted in Jesus's victory and the questioners' public humiliation or silence. The cessation of questioning marks the end of their active challenge strategy — they shift to the passive surveillance (v.20's spies) and will ultimately resort to betrayal (22:3-6) rather than further direct confrontation. Jesus's rhetorical dominance in the temple has been total.
41: 'But he said to them, How can they say that the Christ is David's son?' — having successfully defended himself against three challenges, Jesus now takes the offensive with his own question about the Messiah's identity. The Davidic descent of the Messiah was a standard expectation (Isa 11:1; Jer 23:5; Ezek 34:23; Pss. Sol. 17.21 — 'Raise up for them their king, the son of David'). Jesus's question does not deny the Davidic descent (cf. Luke 1:32-33, 69; 2:4; 18:38-39) but challenges the adequacy of understanding the Messiah only in Davidic-son terms.
42: 'For David himself says in the Book of Psalms, The Lord said to my Lord, Sit at my right hand' — Psalm 110:1 (LXX: eipen ho Kyrios tō Kyriō mou) was the most-cited OT text in early Christianity (Acts 2:34-35; 1 Cor 15:25; Heb 1:13; 10:13). Its attribution to David ('David himself says') makes the first 'Lord' (YHWH/God) speaking to a second 'Lord' (David's lord, the enthroned figure). David's son would normally be David's inferior — a son does not call his father 'my lord.' But David calls the messianic figure his lord, implying the Messiah transcends the father-son hierarchy.
43: 'Until I make your enemies your footstool' — the footstool-imagery (Ps 110:1b) reflects ancient Near Eastern royal iconography where conquered enemies were depicted prostrate under the victorious king's feet (Ps 8:6; Josh 10:24; 1 Kgs 5:3). The session 'at the right hand' (ek dexiōn mou) is the place of highest honor and authority in ancient Near Eastern court protocol — second only to the sovereign. The Psalm's promise to the enthroned figure is thus both a seat of honor and a guarantee of ultimate victory over all opposition.
44: 'David thus calls him Lord, so how is he his son?' — the question is a rhetorical puzzle (not a denial of Davidic descent) that forces hearers to reckon with the Messiah's identity beyond the merely human-royal category. The answer implicit in Luke's Gospel (and made explicit in Acts 2:36) is that Jesus is both David's son (by physical descent, 1:32) and David's Lord (by his resurrection and enthronement at God's right hand). The Davidic-son category is true but insufficient — the Messiah is simultaneously David's descendant and David's Lord, which requires both incarnation and divine identity to explain.
45: 'And in the hearing of all the people he said to his disciples, Beware of the scribes' — the public warning against scribes 'in the hearing of all the people' is a deliberate public act of prophetic denunciation. The explicit audience (all the people, the disciples) creates maximum public visibility for the indictment — this is not a private warning but a public honor-challenge to the scribal establishment. Jesus's willingness to issue public denunciations of the scribes while in the temple — their institutional home — demonstrates the boldness that has characterized the entire temple week.
46: 'Who like to walk around in long robes, and love greetings in the marketplaces and the best seats in the synagogues and the places of honor at feasts' — the four status-behaviors criticized (long robes, marketplace greetings, synagogue seats, feast places) are all honor-seeking behaviors in Greco-Roman and Jewish public culture. Long robes (stolai — formal robes worn by Roman officials and Jewish priestly-scribal figures) signaled high status. Marketplace greetings with proper titles (Rabbi, Abba, Teacher) were status performances. The best seat in the synagogue (facing the congregation from the front) and the honored couch at feasts were coveted positions of public visibility.
47: 'Who devour widows' houses and for a pretense make long prayers. They will receive the greater condemnation' — the two charges bracket each other: economic predation of the vulnerable (widows = the paradigmatic defenseless category in both Jewish and Greco-Roman law: Exod 22:22; Deut 10:18; Isa 1:17; Zech 7:10) combined with religious performance (long prayers as social performance rather than genuine petition). The condemnation (krima) they receive is not merely equal to their crimes but greater — their religious authority and knowledge amplifies their accountability (cf. Luke 12:47-48 on graduated judgment).
